examples.explanation: Call knapsack(0,7): i=0, weight=1≤7, skip=knapsack(1,7), include=1+knapsack(1,6). knapsack(1,7): i=1, weight=3≤7, skip=knapsack(2,7), include=4+knapsack(2,4). knapsack(2,7): i=2, weight=4≤7, skip=knapsack(3,7), include=5+knapsack(3,3). knapsack(3,7): i=3, weight=5≤7, skip=knapsack(4,7)=0, include=7+knapsack(4,2)=7+0=7, max(0,7)=7. So knapsack(2,7): skip=7, include=5+knapsack(3,3). knapsack(3,3): i=3, weight=5>3, skip=knapsack(4,3)=0, include not possible, max=0. So include=5+0=5, max(7,5)=7. knapsack(1,7): skip=7, include=4+knapsack(2,4). knapsack(2,4): i=2, weight=4≤4, skip=knapsack(3,4), include=5+knapsack(3,0). knapsack(3,4): i=3, weight=5>4, skip=knapsack(4,4)=0, max=0. knapsack(3,0): i=3, weight=5>0, skip=0, max=0. So knapsack(2,4): skip=0, include=5+0=5, max=5. So include=4+5=9, max(7,9)=9. knapsack(0,7): skip=9, include=1+knapsack(1,6). knapsack(1,6): i=1, weight=3≤6, skip=knapsack(2,6), include=4+knapsack(2,3). knapsack(2,6): i=2, weight=4≤6, skip=knapsack(3,6), include=5+knapsack(3,2). knapsack(3,6): i=3, weight=5≤6, skip=knapsack(4,6)=0, include=7+knapsack(4,1)=7+0=7, max=7. knapsack(3,2): i=3, weight=5>2, skip=0, max=0. So knapsack(2,6): skip=7, include=5+0=5, max=7. knapsack(2,3): i=2, weight=4>3, skip=knapsack(3,3)=0, max=0. So knapsack(1,6): skip=7, include=4+0=4, max=7. So include=1+7=8, max(9,8)=9. Final output 9.

Call knapsack(0,4): i=0, weight=2≤4, skip=knapsack(1,4), include=10+knapsack(1,2). knapsack(1,4): i=1, weight=1≤4, skip=knapsack(2,4), include=20+knapsack(2,3). knapsack(2,4): i=2, weight=3≤4, skip=knapsack(3,4)=0, include=30+knapsack(3,1)=30+0=30, max=30. knapsack(2,3): i=2, weight=3≤3, skip=knapsack(3,3)=0, include=30+knapsack(3,0)=30+0=30, max=30. So knapsack(1,4): skip=30, include=20+30=50, max=50. knapsack(1,2): i=1, weight=1≤2, skip=knapsack(2,2), include=20+knapsack(2,1). knapsack(2,2): i=2, weight=3>2, skip=knapsack(3,2)=0, max=0. knapsack(2,1): i=2, weight=3>1, skip=0, max=0. So knapsack(1,2): skip=0, include=20+0=20, max=20. So knapsack(0,4): skip=50, include=10+20=30, max=50. Final output 50.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Generate all subsets of items by recursively including or excluding each item, checking if the total weight fits within the knapsack capacity. This approach explores every possible combination, making it simple but inefficient for larger inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — 0/1 Knapsack with Brute Force Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution exploring all subsets to maximize value within weight capacity.
ladders.explanation: The 0/1 Knapsack problem requires selecting items with given weights and values to maximize total value without exceeding a weight capacity, where each item can be taken at most once. The brute force recursion approach systematically explores all possible subsets of items using a depth-first search. At each recursive call for an item index, we have two choices: include the item (if it fits) or exclude it. This generates a binary decision tree with 2^n leaf nodes representing all subsets. The intuition is that by exploring every combination, we guarantee finding the optimal solution, though this becomes impractical for large n due to exponential time complexity. The algorithm starts from the first item and recurses through all items, tracking remaining capacity. When we reach beyond the last item, we return 0 (base case). For each item, we compute two values: one if we skip the item (recursive call with next index and same capacity), and one if we include it (if weight ≤ capacity, add its value plus recursive call with next index and reduced capacity). The maximum of these two is returned. Time complexity is O(2^n) as we explore all subsets, with space O(n) for recursion depth. This connects to L1 as L0 implements the complete recursive function described there. Common mistakes include not checking weight before inclusion (leading to invalid states), forgetting base cases, or inefficiently recomputing values (though memoization is not used here). Real code pattern: def knapsack(i, cap): if i == n: return 0; skip = knapsack(i+1, cap); include = 0; if weights[i] <= cap: include = values[i] + knapsack(i+1, cap-weights[i]); return max(skip, include).

examples.trace: Call knapsack(0,7): i=0, weight=1≤7, skip=knapsack(1,7), include=1+knapsack(1,6). knapsack(1,7): i=1, weight=3≤7, skip=knapsack(2,7), include=4+knapsack(2,4). knapsack(2,7): i=2, weight=4≤7, skip=knapsack(3,7), include=5+knapsack(3,3). knapsack(3,7): i=3, weight=5≤7, skip=knapsack(4,7)=0, include=7+knapsack(4,2)=7+0=7, max(0,7)=7. So knapsack(2,7): skip=7, include=5+knapsack(3,3). knapsack(3,3): i=3, weight=5>3, skip=knapsack(4,3)=0, include not possible, max=0. So include=5+0=5, max(7,5)=7. knapsack(1,7): skip=7, include=4+knapsack(2,4). knapsack(2,4): i=2, weight=4≤4, skip=knapsack(3,4), include=5+knapsack(3,0). knapsack(3,4): i=3, weight=5>4, skip=knapsack(4,4)=0, max=0. knapsack(3,0): i=3, weight=5>0, skip=0, max=0. So knapsack(2,4): skip=0, include=5+0=5, max=5. So include=4+5=9, max(7,9)=9. knapsack(0,7): skip=9, include=1+knapsack(1,6). knapsack(1,6): i=1, weight=3≤6, skip=knapsack(2,6), include=4+knapsack(2,3). knapsack(2,6): i=2, weight=4≤6, skip=knapsack(3,6), include=5+knapsack(3,2). knapsack(3,6): i=3, weight=5≤6, skip=knapsack(4,6)=0, include=7+knapsack(4,1)=7+0=7, max=7. knapsack(3,2): i=3, weight=5>2, skip=0, max=0. So knapsack(2,6): skip=7, include=5+0=5, max=7. knapsack(2,3): i=2, weight=4>3, skip=knapsack(3,3)=0, max=0. So knapsack(1,6): skip=7, include=4+0=4, max=7. So include=1+7=8, max(9,8)=9. Final output 9.

Call knapsack(0,4): i=0, weight=2≤4, skip=knapsack(1,4), include=10+knapsack(1,2). knapsack(1,4): i=1, weight=1≤4, skip=knapsack(2,4), include=20+knapsack(2,3). knapsack(2,4): i=2, weight=3≤4, skip=knapsack(3,4)=0, include=30+knapsack(3,1)=30+0=30, max=30. knapsack(2,3): i=2, weight=3≤3, skip=knapsack(3,3)=0, include=30+knapsack(3,0)=30+0=30, max=30. So knapsack(1,4): skip=30, include=20+30=50, max=50. knapsack(1,2): i=1, weight=1≤2, skip=knapsack(2,2), include=20+knapsack(2,1). knapsack(2,2): i=2, weight=3>2, skip=knapsack(3,2)=0, max=0. knapsack(2,1): i=2, weight=3>1, skip=0, max=0. So knapsack(1,2): skip=0, include=20+0=20, max=20. So knapsack(0,4): skip=50, include=10+20=30, max=50. Final output 50.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Decision Function for Item Inclusion
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that decides to include or exclude an item at a given index.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach: a recursive function that, for a given item index and remaining capacity, returns the maximum value achievable from the remaining items. The intuition is to break the problem into smaller subproblems: at each step, we decide whether to take the current item or not, then recursively solve for the rest. This embodies the optimal substructure property of the knapsack problem. The algorithm: function knapsack(i, cap): if i equals number of items n, return 0 (base case, no items left). Otherwise, compute skip = knapsack(i+1, cap) (value if we exclude item i). Compute include = 0; if weight[i] ≤ cap, then include = value[i] + knapsack(i+1, cap - weight[i]) (value if we include item i). Return max(skip, include). Time complexity for this function on its own is O(2^(n-i)) in worst case, but when integrated into L0, it becomes O(2^n). Space is O(n-i) for recursion stack. This connects to L0 as L1 provides the recursive function that L0 calls with initial parameters i=0, cap=capacity. Common mistakes include incorrect base case (e.g., returning 0 when cap=0, which is wrong because items might have zero weight), or not validating weight before inclusion. Real code pattern: def knapsack(i, cap): if i == len(weights): return 0; exclude = knapsack(i+1, cap); include = 0; if weights[i] <= cap: include = values[i] + knapsack(i+1, cap - weights[i]); return max(exclude, include).

examples.trace: Call knapsack(0,5): i=0, weight=2≤5, skip=knapsack(1,5), include=10+knapsack(1,3). knapsack(1,5): i=1, weight=3≤5, skip=knapsack(2,5), include=20+knapsack(2,2). knapsack(2,5): i=2, weight=4≤5, skip=knapsack(3,5)=0, include=30+knapsack(3,1)=30+0=30, max=30. knapsack(2,2): i=2, weight=4>2, skip=knapsack(3,2)=0, max=0. So knapsack(1,5): skip=30, include=20+0=20, max=30. knapsack(1,3): i=1, weight=3≤3, skip=knapsack(2,3), include=20+knapsack(2,0). knapsack(2,3): i=2, weight=4>3, skip=knapsack(3,3)=0, max=0. knapsack(2,0): i=2, weight=4>0, skip=0, max=0. So knapsack(1,3): skip=0, include=20+0=20, max=20. So knapsack(0,5): skip=30, include=10+20=30, max=30.

Call knapsack(1,4): i=1, weight=2≤4, skip=knapsack(2,4), include=10+knapsack(2,2). knapsack(2,4): i=2, weight=3≤4, skip=knapsack(3,4)=0, include=15+knapsack(3,1)=15+0=15, max=15. knapsack(2,2): i=2, weight=3>2, skip=knapsack(3,2)=0, max=0. So knapsack(1,4): skip=15, include=10+0=10, max=15.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Exploring All Subsets via Binary Decisions
ladders.desc: Understand how recursive inclusion/exclusion generates all possible subsets of items.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the brute force approach is generating all 2^n subsets of n items through binary decisions at each step. This is achieved by a recursive tree where each node represents a state (current item index, remaining capacity), and each edge represents a decision: include the item or exclude it. The intuition is that any subset can be uniquely represented by a sequence of n binary choices, and the recursion systematically enumerates all such sequences. For example, with items [A,B,C], the recursion explores subsets: {}, {A}, {B}, {C}, {A,B}, {A,C}, {B,C}, {A,B,C}. The algorithm: at each call for item i, we branch into two recursive calls: one excluding item i (move to i+1 with same capacity), and one including item i if it fits (move to i+1 with capacity reduced by weight[i]). The base case when i reaches n terminates a path. Time complexity arises from 2^n leaf nodes, each corresponding to a subset. Space is O(n) for recursion depth. This connects to L1 as L2 explains the branching mechanism that L1 implements. Common mistakes include not pruning invalid branches (when weight exceeds capacity, include branch is skipped), or misunderstanding that the tree explores all combinations even if some are infeasible due to weight. Real code pattern: The recursion inherently does this; visualize as a tree traversal.

examples.trace: Start at i=0, cap=2. Branch exclude: i=1, cap=2. From i=1, cap=2: exclude leads to i=2 (base, subset {}), include if weight=2≤2 leads to i=2, cap=0 (subset {item1}). Back to i=0, cap=2: branch include if weight=1≤2: i=1, cap=1. From i=1, cap=1: exclude leads to i=2 (subset {item0}), include not possible as weight=2>1. So subsets: {} (value 0), {item1} (value 20), {item0} (value 10), {item0,item1} not feasible. Max value 20.
Start at i=0, cap=5. Branch exclude: i=1 (base, subset {}). Branch include if weight=3≤5: i=1, cap=2 (subset {item0}). Subsets: {} value 0, {item0} value 5. Max 5.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Function with Parameters
ladders.desc: Write a recursive function that takes an index and capacity, returning a value based on decisions.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on creating a recursive function that uses parameters to track state: the current item index and the remaining knapsack capacity. The intuition is that recursion allows us to break down the problem into smaller instances by reducing the index (moving to next item) and possibly the capacity (if an item is included). The function signature is knapsack(i, cap), where i is the index of the item to consider (0-based), and cap is the remaining weight capacity. The base case is when i is out of bounds (i == n), return 0. Otherwise, we compute two values recursively: one for excluding the item (call knapsack(i+1, cap)), and one for including it only if its weight ≤ cap (call knapsack(i+1, cap - weight[i]) and add value[i]). The function returns the maximum of these two. Time complexity for a single call depends on the recursion tree; in practice, it's part of the O(2^n) overall. Space is O(n) due to recursion depth. This connects to L2 as L3 implements the recursive function that performs the binary decisions described in L2. Common mistakes include using global variables incorrectly, off-by-one errors in base case, or not passing updated parameters correctly. Real code pattern: def knapsack(i, cap): if i >= n: return 0; exclude = knapsack(i+1, cap); include = 0; if weights[i] <= cap: include = values[i] + knapsack(i+1, cap - weights[i]); return max(exclude, include).

examples.trace: Call knapsack(0,3): i=0, weight=1≤3, exclude=knapsack(1,3), include=10+knapsack(1,2). knapsack(1,3): i=1, weight=2≤3, exclude=knapsack(2,3)=0, include=20+knapsack(2,1)=20+0=20, max=20. knapsack(1,2): i=1, weight=2≤2, exclude=knapsack(2,2)=0, include=20+knapsack(2,0)=20+0=20, max=20. So knapsack(0,3): exclude=20, include=10+20=30, max=30.

Call knapsack(1,1): i=1, weight=1≤1, exclude=knapsack(2,1), include=10+knapsack(2,0). knapsack(2,1): i=2, weight=3>1, exclude=knapsack(3,1)=0, max=0. knapsack(2,0): i=2, weight=3>0, exclude=0, max=0. So knapsack(1,1): exclude=0, include=10+0=10, max=10.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Conditional Inclusion with Weight Check
ladders.desc: Check if an item fits in remaining capacity and compute value if included.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is a key step in the recursion: for a given item i and remaining capacity cap, we conditionally include the item only if its weight is less than or equal to cap. The intuition is that we cannot include an item if it exceeds the capacity, as that would violate the knapsack constraint. The operation involves: first, check if weight[i] ≤ cap. If true, we can include the item, which contributes its value value[i] and reduces the capacity by weight[i] for subsequent decisions. If false, inclusion is not possible, so we treat the include value as 0 (or skip it). In pseudocode: if weights[i] <= cap: include_value = values[i] + recursive_call(i+1, cap - weights[i]); else: include_value = 0. This ensures we only explore feasible subsets. Time complexity is O(1) for this check, but it influences the recursion tree by pruning invalid branches. Space is O(1). This connects to L3 as L4 is the conditional logic inside the recursive function of L3. Common mistakes include using strict inequality (< instead of ≤), or not handling the else case, leading to incorrect values. Real code pattern: if weights[idx] <= remaining_cap: included = values[idx] + knapsack(idx+1, remaining_cap - weights[idx]) else: included = 0.

examples.trace: Check: weight 4 ≤ cap 5? Yes. So include is possible. New cap after inclusion: 5 - 4 = 1. Include value = 10 + value from subsequent items (computed recursively).
Check: weight 6 ≤ cap 4? No. So include is not possible. Include value = 0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Recursion with Base Case
ladders.desc: Learn recursive function calls that terminate with a base case.
ladders.explanation: Recursion is a programming technique where a function calls itself to solve smaller instances of the same problem. The foundation for the knapsack brute force approach is understanding how recursion works with a base case that stops the recursion. The intuition is to break a problem into similar subproblems until a trivial case (base case) is reached. For example, to compute factorial(n), we define factorial(n) = n * factorial(n-1) with base case factorial(0)=1. In knapsack, the base case is when no items are left to consider (index out of bounds), where the maximum value is 0. The algorithm: define a function f(i) that solves the problem for items from i to end. In the body, if i is at the end (base case), return a simple value. Otherwise, make recursive calls to f(i+1) and combine results. Time and space depend on recursion depth; here, depth is n. This connects to L4 as L5 provides the recursive structure that L4 operates within. Common mistakes include missing base case (leading to infinite recursion), or not reducing problem size in recursive calls. Real code pattern: def recursive_func(param): if base_case_condition: return base_value; else: result = combine(param, recursive_func(modified_param)); return result.

examples.trace: Define sum(arr, i): if i == len(arr): return 0 (base case). Else: return arr[i] + sum(arr, i+1). Call sum([2,3,4], 0): i=0, return 2 + sum([2,3,4], 1). sum([2,3,4], 1): return 3 + sum([2,3,4], 2). sum([2,3,4], 2): return 4 + sum([2,3,4], 3). sum([2,3,4], 3): base case, return 0. So back: 4+0=4, then 3+4=7, then 2+7=9.

Define countdown(n): if n < 0: return (base case). Else: print(n), countdown(n-1). Call countdown(3): print 3, call countdown(2). countdown(2): print 2, call countdown(1). countdown(1): print 1, call countdown(0). countdown(0): print 0, call countdown(-1) which returns. Output sequence: 3,2,1,0.

approaches.name: DP with Memoization (Top-Down) O(n*W)
approaches.summary: Use recursion with memoization to store results of subproblems (item index and remaining capacity) in a hash table or array, avoiding redundant calculations. This top-down approach builds the solution by breaking the problem into overlapping subproblems.

ladders.title: Full Problem — 0/1 Knapsack with DP Memoization
ladders.desc: Complete solution using recursion with memoization to maximize value within capacity.
ladders.explanation: The 0/1 Knapsack problem requires selecting items with given weights and values to maximize total value without exceeding capacity W, where each item can be taken at most once. The DP with memoization approach solves this by breaking it into overlapping subproblems: for each item i and remaining capacity w, we decide whether to include item i or not. The intuition is that optimal solution for (i,w) depends on optimal solutions for (i+1,w) (skip item) and (i+1,w-weight[i]) (include item). We use a memo table (dictionary or 2D array) to store computed results, avoiding exponential recomputation. Step-by-step: 1. Define recursive function knapsack(i,w) returning max value from items i..n-1 with capacity w. 2. Base cases: if i==n (no items left) return 0; if w<=0 return 0. 3. If (i,w) in memo, return memo[(i,w)]. 4. If weight[i] > w, cannot include item: result = knapsack(i+1,w). 5. Else, result = max(knapsack(i+1,w), value[i] + knapsack(i+1,w-weight[i])). 6. Store result in memo and return. Time: O(n*W) as each (i,w) pair computed once. Space: O(n*W) for memo. Connection to L1: This uses the key recursive subroutine from L1. Common mistakes: forgetting base cases, incorrect memo key, not handling negative capacity. Real code pattern: def knapsack(i,w): if i==n: return 0; if w<=0: return 0; if (i,w) in memo: return memo[(i,w)]; if weight[i]>w: res=knapsack(i+1,w); else: res=max(knapsack(i+1,w), value[i]+knapsack(i+1,w-weight[i])); memo[(i,w)]=res; return res.

examples.trace: Call knapsack(0,5). i=0,w=5, weight[0]=1<=5, compute include=10+knapsack(1,4), skip=knapsack(1,5). knapsack(1,4): weight[1]=2<=4, include=15+knapsack(2,2), skip=knapsack(2,4). knapsack(2,2): weight[2]=3>2, skip=knapsack(3,2)=0. knapsack(2,4): weight[2]=3<=4, include=40+knapsack(3,1)=40+0=40, skip=knapsack(3,4)=0, max=40. Back to knapsack(1,4): include=15+0=15, skip=40, max=40. knapsack(1,5): weight[1]=2<=5, include=15+knapsack(2,3), skip=knapsack(2,5). knapsack(2,3): weight[2]=3<=3, include=40+knapsack(3,0)=40+0=40, skip=0, max=40. knapsack(2,5): weight[2]=3<=5, include=40+knapsack(3,2)=40+0=40, skip=0, max=40. So knapsack(1,5): include=15+40=55, skip=40, max=55. Back to knapsack(0,5): include=10+40=50, skip=55, max=55. Return 55.

Call knapsack(0,5). i=0,w=5, weight[0]=2<=5, include=3+knapsack(1,3), skip=knapsack(1,5). knapsack(1,3): weight[1]=3<=3, include=4+knapsack(2,0)=4+0=4, skip=knapsack(2,3). knapsack(2,3): weight[2]=4>3, skip=knapsack(3,3). knapsack(3,3): weight[3]=5>3, skip=knapsack(4,3)=0. So knapsack(2,3)=0, knapsack(1,3)=max(4,0)=4. knapsack(1,5): weight[1]=3<=5, include=4+knapsack(2,2), skip=knapsack(2,5). knapsack(2,2): weight[2]=4>2, skip=knapsack(3,2). knapsack(3,2): weight[3]=5>2, skip=0. So knapsack(2,2)=0. knapsack(2,5): weight[2]=4<=5, include=5+knapsack(3,1), skip=knapsack(3,5). knapsack(3,1): weight[3]=5>1, skip=0. knapsack(3,5): weight[3]=5<=5, include=6+knapsack(4,0)=6+0=6, skip=0, max=6. So knapsack(2,5)=max(5+0=5,6)=6. Back to knapsack(1,5): include=4+0=4, skip=6, max=6. Back to knapsack(0,5): include=3+4=7, skip=6, max=7. Return 7.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Decision Function with Memoization
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that decides to include or skip an item, using memoization.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the top-down DP approach. It defines a function f(i,w) that returns the maximum value achievable from items starting at index i with remaining capacity w. The intuition is that at each step, we have two choices: skip the current item (move to next item with same capacity) or include it (add its value and reduce capacity by its weight). By memoizing results in a dictionary or 2D array, we avoid recomputing the same subproblems, turning exponential recursion into polynomial time. Step-by-step: 1. Check base cases: if i is beyond last item (i==n) or capacity w<=0, return 0. 2. Check memo table: if (i,w) already computed, return stored value. 3. If weight[i] > w, cannot include: result = f(i+1,w). 4. Else, result = max(f(i+1,w), value[i] + f(i+1,w-weight[i])). 5. Store result in memo and return. Time: O(1) per call with memo hit, but overall setup leads to O(n*W). Space: O(n*W) for memo storage. Connection to L0: This function is called recursively in L0 to solve the full problem. Common mistakes: incorrect memo key (e.g., using w only), not handling base cases for negative capacity. Real code pattern: memo={}; def f(i,w): if i>=n or w<=0: return 0; if (i,w) in memo: return memo[(i,w)]; if weights[i]>w: res=f(i+1,w); else: res=max(f(i+1,w), values[i]+f(i+1,w-weights[i])); memo[(i,w)]=res; return res.

examples.trace: Call f(0,3). i=0,w=3, weight[0]=1<=3, compute include=5+f(1,2), skip=f(1,3). f(1,2): i=1,w=2, weight[1]=2<=2, include=3+f(2,0)=3+0=3, skip=f(2,2)=0, max=3. f(1,3): i=1,w=3, weight[1]=2<=3, include=3+f(2,1)=3+0=3, skip=f(2,3)=0, max=3. Back to f(0,3): include=5+3=8, skip=3, max=8. But wait, correct: f(1,2)=3, f(1,3)=3, so f(0,3)=max(5+3=8,3)=8. However, with n=2, items are [5,3], weights [1,2], capacity 3: best is take both (5+3=8). Output should be 8. Let me recompute: f(0,3): weight[0]=1<=3, include=5+f(1,2). f(1,2): weight[1]=2<=2, include=3+f(2,0)=3+0=3, skip=f(2,2)=0, so f(1,2)=3. Skip option for f(0,3) is f(1,3). f(1,3): weight[1]=2<=3, include=3+f(2,1)=3+0=3, skip=f(2,3)=0, so f(1,3)=3. Thus f(0,3)=max(5+3=8,3)=8. I see error in initial output; correcting to 8.

Call f(1,4). i=1,w=4, weight[1]=2<=4, compute include=5+f(2,2), skip=f(2,4). f(2,2): i=2,w=2, weight[2]=4>2, so skip=f(3,2)=0. f(2,4): i=2,w=4, weight[2]=4<=4, include=6+f(3,0)=6+0=6, skip=f(3,4)=0, max=6. Back to f(1,4): include=5+0=5, skip=6, max=6. Return 6.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Optimal Substructure and Overlapping Subproblems
ladders.desc: Understand how the problem breaks into smaller subproblems with overlapping solutions.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of DP for 0/1 Knapsack relies on two key properties: optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems. Optimal substructure means the optimal solution to the original problem can be constructed from optimal solutions to its subproblems. For knapsack, the max value for items i..n-1 with capacity w is max of: 1) skipping item i (solution for items i+1..n-1 with same capacity w), or 2) including item i (value[i] plus solution for items i+1..n-1 with reduced capacity w-weight[i]). This defines a recurrence relation. Overlapping subproblems occur because multiple recursive calls may solve the same (i,w) pair; e.g., f(i,w) might be called from different paths. Memoization exploits this by storing results to avoid recomputation. Intuition: Imagine a decision tree where each node represents a state (i,w); many branches lead to same states, so caching results drastically reduces work. Step-by-step: 1. Define state (i,w). 2. Recurrence: f(i,w) = 0 if i==n or w<=0; f(i,w) = f(i+1,w) if weight[i]>w; else f(i,w) = max(f(i+1,w), value[i]+f(i+1,w-weight[i])). 3. Use memo to store f(i,w). Time: Without memo, exponential O(2^n); with memo, O(n*W). Space: O(n*W) for memo. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the recursive function. Common mistakes: misunderstanding optimal substructure (e.g., thinking greedy works), not identifying overlapping subproblems. Real code pattern: The recurrence is directly translated into the recursive function with memo.

examples.trace: States: (0,5): max of skip f(1,5) or include 4+f(1,3). (1,5): max of skip f(2,5)=0 or include 5+f(2,2)=5+0=5, so 5. (1,3): max of skip f(2,3)=0 or include 5+f(2,0)=5+0=5, so 5. Thus (0,5): max(5,4+5=9)=9. Overlap: f(1,3) and f(1,5) are distinct, but f(2,2) etc. are base cases reused.

States: (0,2): max of skip f(1,2) or include 2+f(1,1). (1,2): max of skip f(2,2) or include 3+f(2,1). (2,2): max of skip f(3,2)=0 or include 4+f(3,1)=4+0=4, so 4. (2,1): max of skip f(3,1)=0 or include 4+f(3,0)=4+0=4, so 4. (1,1): max of skip f(2,1) or include 3+f(2,0)=3+0=3. f(2,1)=4, so max(4,3)=4. Back: f(1,2)=max(4,3+4=7)=7. f(1,1)=4. Then f(0,2)=max(7,2+4=6)=7. Overlap: f(2,1) computed once and reused.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursion with State Parameters
ladders.desc: Implement a recursive function that explores decisions based on item index and capacity.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on creating a recursive function that takes two parameters: current item index i and remaining capacity w, and returns the best value from that state. It's a simpler version without memoization, emphasizing the decision process. The intuition is to simulate all possible combinations of items by recursively choosing to include or skip each item, respecting capacity constraints. This brute-force recursion has exponential time but teaches the fundamental decision tree. Step-by-step: 1. Define function explore(i,w). 2. Base case: if i==n (no items left) or w<=0, return 0. 3. If weight[i] > w, cannot include: return explore(i+1,w). 4. Else, return max(explore(i+1,w), value[i] + explore(i+1,w-weight[i])). This explores two branches: skipping item i or including it. Time: O(2^n) in worst case, as it explores all subsets. Space: O(n) for recursion depth. Connection to L2: This implements the recurrence relation from core logic, but without optimization. Common mistakes: infinite recursion if base cases missing, incorrect parameter updates. Real code pattern: def explore(i,w): if i==n or w<=0: return 0; if weights[i]>w: return explore(i+1,w); return max(explore(i+1,w), values[i]+explore(i+1,w-weights[i])).

examples.trace: Call explore(0,4). i=0,w=4, weight[0]=1<=4, compute include=5+explore(1,3), skip=explore(1,4). explore(1,3): i=1,w=3, weight[1]=3<=3, include=7+explore(2,0)=7+0=7, skip=explore(2,3)=0, max=7. explore(1,4): i=1,w=4, weight[1]=3<=4, include=7+explore(2,1)=7+0=7, skip=explore(2,4)=0, max=7. Back to explore(0,4): include=5+7=12, skip=7, max=12. Wait, correct: with weights [1,3], values [5,7], capacity 4, best is take both (5+7=12). Output should be 12. I see error; correcting trace: explore(1,3)=7, explore(1,4)=7, so explore(0,4)=max(5+7=12,7)=12.

Call explore(0,2). i=0,w=2, weight[0]=2<=2, include=3+explore(1,0), skip=explore(1,2). explore(1,0): i=1,w=0, base case return 0. explore(1,2): i=1,w=2, weight[1]=1<=2, include=2+explore(2,1)=2+0=2, skip=explore(2,2)=0, max=2. Back to explore(0,2): include=3+0=3, skip=2, max=3. Return 3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Choosing Maximum of Two Options
ladders.desc: Compute the maximum value between including and excluding an item.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is a simple comparison: given the value from skipping an item and the value from including it (if possible), choose the larger. It's fundamental to the decision process in knapsack. The intuition is that at each step, we want the best outcome, so we pick the option yielding higher total value. Including an item adds its value but consumes capacity; skipping preserves capacity for other items. This operation is applied recursively. Step-by-step: For current item i with weight weight[i] and value value[i], and remaining capacity w: 1. If weight[i] > w, cannot include, so result is skip_value (from skipping item). 2. Else, compute include_value = value[i] + best_value_with_reduced_capacity. 3. Result = max(skip_value, include_value). This requires precomputed skip_value and include_value from recursive calls. Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used in the recursive function to decide between branches. Common mistakes: comparing values without considering capacity, incorrect addition. Real code pattern: if weight[i] > w: return skip_value; else: return max(skip_value, value[i] + include_value).

examples.trace: Weight 3 <= capacity 4, so include is possible. include_value = 4 + best_value_with_reduced_capacity, but here include_value is given as 8. So max(5,8)=8. Choose include.
Weight 6 > capacity 5, cannot include. So result is skip_value=10.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Value and Weight Constraints
ladders.desc: Grasp basic idea of selecting items with values and weights under a capacity limit.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept introduces the core problem setup: we have items, each with a weight and a value, and a knapsack with a maximum weight capacity. The goal is to choose a subset of items to maximize total value without exceeding the capacity. It's a simple selection problem with a constraint. Intuition: Think of packing a bag with items of different weights and values; you want the most valuable combination that fits. This involves basic arithmetic: summing weights and values, and comparing to capacity. Step-by-step: 1. List items with weights and values. 2. Consider subsets of items. 3. For each subset, compute total weight = sum of weights, total value = sum of values. 4. If total weight <= capacity, it's a valid subset. 5. Choose valid subset with highest total value. This brute-force approach checks all subsets (2^n possibilities), which is inefficient but easy to understand. Time: O(2^n) for enumeration. Space: O(n) to store subsets. Connection to L4: This concept leads to the decision operation of including or skipping items. Common mistakes: confusing weight and value, misunderstanding capacity constraint. Real code pattern: Simple iteration over subsets using bitmasks or recursion to compute totals.

examples.trace: Subsets: {}: weight=0, value=0, valid. {item1}: weight=2, value=3, valid. {item2}: weight=3, value=4, valid. {item1,item2}: weight=5, value=7, valid. Max value among valid subsets is 7 from both items.
Subsets: {}: weight=0, value=0, valid. {item1}: weight=5, value=10, invalid (5>4). Max valid value is 0.

approaches.name: DP with Tabulation (Bottom-Up) O(n*W)
approaches.summary: Iteratively fill a 2D DP table where dp[i][w] represents the maximum value achievable with the first i items and capacity w. This bottom-up method computes solutions for all subproblems in a systematic order, leading to the final answer.

ladders.title: Full Problem — 0/1 Knapsack with Tabulation
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DP tabulation to maximize value within capacity
ladders.explanation: The 0/1 Knapsack problem requires selecting items with given weights and values to maximize total value without exceeding capacity W, where each item can be taken at most once. The DP tabulation approach builds a 2D table dp[i][w] representing the maximum value achievable using the first i items with capacity w. Intuitively, for each item i and capacity w, we have two choices: either skip item i (dp[i-1][w]) or take it if weight[i-1] ≤ w, adding its value to dp[i-1][w-weight[i-1]]. The recurrence is dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], value[i-1] + dp[i-1][w-weight[i-1]]). This bottom-up method systematically fills the table from base cases (dp[0][w]=0 for all w, dp[i][0]=0 for all i) to the final answer dp[n][W]. Time complexity is O(n*W) as we fill n+1 rows and W+1 columns. Space complexity is O(n*W) for the table. Connection to L1: This uses the key recurrence relation to compute all subproblems. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices (items are 1-indexed in dp but 0-indexed in arrays) and incorrectly handling weight bounds. Real code pattern: Initialize dp table with zeros, iterate i from 1 to n and w from 1 to W, apply recurrence, return dp[n][W].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Recurrence for Item Decision
ladders.desc: Compute maximum value for a single item and capacity using previous row
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the core recurrence relation for the knapsack problem: given a DP table row for i-1 items, compute dp[i][w] for item i and capacity w. The intuition is that at each step, we decide whether to include item i (if its weight fits) or exclude it, maximizing value. The algorithm: For a fixed i and w, if weight[i-1] > w, we cannot take the item, so dp[i][w] = dp[i-1][w]. Otherwise, dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], value[i-1] + dp[i-1][w-weight[i-1]]). This represents the optimal substructure property. Time complexity is O(1) per cell, as it involves constant-time comparisons and array accesses. Space complexity is O(1) extra beyond the table. Connection to L0: This recurrence is applied repeatedly in nested loops to fill the entire table. Common mistakes include using wrong indices for weights/values (0-indexed) or accessing dp out of bounds when w-weight[i-1] is negative. Real code pattern: if w < weight[i-1]: dp[i][w] = dp[i-1][w]; else: dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], value[i-1] + dp[i-1][w-weight[i-1]]).

examples.trace: Given prev_row for i=1: dp[1][w] values for w=0..4 are [0,0,0,0,0]. For i=2, weight=2, value=15. Since weight=2 ≤ w=3, compute: option1 = dp[1][3] = 0 (exclude), option2 = 15 + dp[1][3-2=1] = 15 + 0 = 15 (include). max(0,15) = 15. So dp[2][3] = 15.
Prev_row for i=2: dp[2][w] = [0,10,10,10,10] for w=0..4. For i=3, weight=3, value=40. Since weight=3 > w=2, cannot include item. So dp[3][2] = dp[2][2] = 10 (exclude only).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Optimal Substructure and DP State Definition
ladders.desc: Define DP state and recurrence using optimal substructure property
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the knapsack problem lies in its optimal substructure: the optimal solution for i items and capacity w can be built from optimal solutions of smaller subproblems. We define state dp[i][w] as the maximum value achievable using the first i items with capacity w. The recurrence arises from considering the last item i: if we don't take it, the value is dp[i-1][w]; if we take it (and it fits), the value is value[i-1] + dp[i-1][w-weight[i-1]]. The maximum of these gives dp[i][w]. Intuitively, this works because any optimal solution either includes item i or doesn't, and in either case, the remainder must be optimal for the reduced problem. This is a classic application of dynamic programming where overlapping subproblems are solved once and reused. Time and space analysis for this logic is conceptual, but it leads to O(n*W) overall. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the recurrence subroutine. Common mistakes include misdefining the state (e.g., using dp[i][w] for items up to i but not necessarily including i) or not handling base cases (dp[0][w]=0, dp[i][0]=0). Real code pattern: State definition as 2D list, recurrence as described.

examples.trace: State: dp[2][3] is max value with first 2 items, capacity 3. From optimal substructure: Option1 (exclude item2) = dp[1][3] = 5. Option2 (include item2) requires weight=2 ≤ 3, so value=4 + dp[1][3-2=1] = 4 + 2 = 6. max(5,6) = 6. So dp[2][3] = 6.
dp[3][4]: Option1 = dp[2][4] = 7. Option2: weight=3 ≤ 4, so 5 + dp[2][4-3=1]. Assume dp[2][1]=3 (given). Then 5+3=8. max(7,8)=8.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Over Items and Capacities
ladders.desc: Nested loops to fill DP table for all subproblems
ladders.explanation: This building block involves the iterative process of filling the DP table using nested loops: outer loop over items i from 1 to n, inner loop over capacities w from 1 to W. For each (i,w), we apply the recurrence to compute dp[i][w] based on previous row dp[i-1][*]. The intuition is that we solve all smaller subproblems in increasing order of i and w, ensuring that when computing dp[i][w], the values dp[i-1][w] and dp[i-1][w-weight[i-1]] are already computed. This systematic progression from base cases (i=0 or w=0) to the final state (i=n, w=W) is key to bottom-up DP. Time complexity for this block is O(n*W) as it performs constant work per cell. Space complexity is O(n*W) for the table. Connection to L2: This iteration implements the optimal substructure repeatedly. Common mistakes include starting loops from 0 instead of 1 (but base cases are set), or iterating w in reverse (not needed here). Real code pattern: for i in range(1, n+1): for w in range(1, W+1): compute dp[i][w].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Max Value Selection with Condition
ladders.desc: Choose maximum between two values based on weight constraint
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves computing the maximum value between two options: excluding an item or including it if its weight does not exceed capacity. Given a current capacity w, item weight wt, item value val, and previous DP values exclude_val (value without item) and include_val_base (value with reduced capacity), we compute include_val = val + include_val_base if wt ≤ w, else it's invalid. Then, result = max(exclude_val, include_val). The intuition is a simple decision rule to maximize gain. This is a fundamental step in many optimization problems. Time complexity is O(1), space O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is performed for each cell in the nested loops. Common mistakes include not checking weight condition before inclusion, or adding val to wrong base value. Real code pattern: if w >= wt: include = val + base; else: include = -inf; result = max(exclude, include).

examples.trace: Since wt=3 ≤ w=4, include option is valid: include_val = val + include_val_base = 7 + 2 = 9. exclude_val = 5. max(5,9) = 9.
wt=5 > w=2, so include option is invalid (cannot take item). Thus, only exclude option: exclude_val = 3. max(3, invalid) = 3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Simple Loops
ladders.desc: Basic operations with arrays and iterative loops
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers fundamental programming concepts: arrays (or lists) to store data like weights and values, and loops to iterate over sequences. For the knapsack problem, we use arrays to hold item weights and values, and nested loops to traverse all items and capacities. Intuitively, arrays allow efficient access to item properties by index, and loops enable systematic processing. For example, to fill a DP table, we use a for loop for items from 1 to n and an inner for loop for capacities from 1 to W. Time complexity depends on loop bounds; here, it introduces O(n*W). Space complexity involves array storage. Connection to L4: These operations are used to apply the max selection repeatedly. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop ranges or incorrect array indexing. Real code pattern: Initialize arrays, use for i in range(n): access weights[i], values[i]; for w in range(W+1): ...

examples.trace: Use a loop: for i in range(3): current_weight = weights[i] (i=0:2, i=1:3, i=2:4), current_value = values[i] (5,6,7). This accesses each item sequentially.
Loop: for w in range(1, 4): w takes values 1, 2, 3. This covers all capacities from 1 to W.

approaches.name: DP with Space Optimization O(n*W)
approaches.summary: Optimize the tabulation approach by using a 1D array to store DP values for the current capacity, updating it in reverse order to avoid overwriting previous states. This reduces space usage while maintaining the same time complexity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — 0/1 Knapsack with Space-Optimized DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution using 1D DP array with reverse iteration to maximize value within weight capacity.
ladders.explanation: The 0/1 Knapsack problem requires selecting items with given weights and values to maximize total value without exceeding a weight capacity W, where each item can be taken at most once. The space-optimized DP approach uses a 1D array dp of size W+1, where dp[w] stores the maximum achievable value for capacity w using items processed so far. We iterate through items, and for each item, we update dp from W down to the item's weight to avoid overwriting states needed for the current item. This works because when processing item i, dp[w] from the previous iteration represents the best value using items 0 to i-1. By updating in reverse, we ensure dp[w - weight[i]] references the previous iteration's state, not an updated one from the current item. Intuition: We're compressing the 2D DP table (items × capacity) into 1D by reusing the array, with reverse iteration preserving the dependency on earlier items. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize dp array of size W+1 with zeros. 2. For each item i from 0 to n-1: a. For capacity w from W down to weight[i]: dp[w] = max(dp[w], dp[w - weight[i]] + value[i]). 3. Return dp[W]. Time: O(n*W) for nested loops. Space: O(W) for the dp array. Connection to L1: This integrates the key subroutine of updating dp in reverse. Common mistakes include iterating forward (which allows using an item multiple times, turning it into an unbounded knapsack) or missing the weight check. Real code pattern: dp = [0]*(W+1); for i in range(n): for w in range(W, weights[i]-1, -1): dp[w] = max(dp[w], dp[w-weights[i]] + values[i]); return dp[W].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Reverse Iteration DP Update
ladders.desc: Update a 1D DP array in reverse order to incorporate a new item without overwriting needed states.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of space-optimized 0/1 Knapsack. Given a DP array dp where dp[w] represents the maximum value achievable with capacity w using items processed so far, and a new item with weight wt and value val, we update dp for capacities from W down to wt. The update rule: dp[w] = max(dp[w], dp[w - wt] + val). Reverse iteration is crucial because dp[w - wt] must refer to the state before considering the current item (i.e., using only previous items). If we iterate forward from wt to W, dp[w - wt] might have already been updated for the current item, allowing the item to be used multiple times (incorrect for 0/1 Knapsack). Intuition: Think of dp as a snapshot of best values after processing certain items; reverse updating ensures we build on the old snapshot. Step-by-step: 1. For w from W down to wt: a. Compute candidate = dp[w - wt] + val. b. Set dp[w] = max(dp[w], candidate). Time: O(W) per item. Space: O(W) for dp. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each item in the full solution. Common mistakes include using forward iteration or missing the weight lower bound. Real code pattern: for w in range(W, wt-1, -1): dp[w] = max(dp[w], dp[w-wt] + val).

ladders.title: Core Logic — 1D DP State Compression
ladders.desc: Reduce 2D DP table to 1D array by reusing space and updating in reverse.
ladders.explanation: In the standard 0/1 Knapsack DP, we use a 2D table dp[i][w] for max value with first i items and capacity w. The recurrence is dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], dp[i-1][w-weight[i]] + value[i]). This shows that row i depends only on row i-1, allowing compression to a 1D array dp[w] that represents the previous row. We iterate items, and for each item, update dp from high to low capacity to avoid using the current item multiple times. Intuition: We're rolling the DP table horizontally, keeping only the latest row, but careful updating order preserves correctness. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize dp[w]=0 for all w. 2. For each item, temporarily treat dp as the previous row, compute new values in reverse, and overwrite dp. Time: O(n*W). Space: O(W). Connection to L1: This logic explains why reverse iteration works. Common mistakes include not initializing dp or confusing with unbounded knapsack. Real code pattern: dp = [0]*(W+1); for each item: for w in reversed(range(weight, W+1)): dp[w] = max(dp[w], dp[w-weight] + value).

testCases.input: weights=[3], values=[8], W=5, after one item
weights=[2,2], values=[5,5], W=4, after two items

ladders.title: Building Block — DP Recurrence for 0/1 Knapsack
ladders.desc: Define the recurrence relation for maximum value with item choices.
ladders.explanation: The recurrence is the mathematical foundation for 0/1 Knapsack DP. Let dp[i][w] be the maximum value achievable using the first i items (indices 0 to i-1) with capacity w. For each item i (0-indexed) with weight wt and value val, we have two choices: skip the item (value dp[i-1][w]) or take it if w >= wt (value dp[i-1][w-wt] + val). Thus, dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], dp[i-1][w-wt] + val) if w >= wt, else dp[i-1][w]. Base cases: dp[0][w] = 0 for all w (no items). Intuition: We build solutions incrementally, combining optimal sub-solutions. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize dp[0][w] = 0. 2. For i from 1 to n: a. For w from 0 to W: i. If w < weight[i-1]: dp[i][w] = dp[i-1][w]. ii. Else: dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], dp[i-1][w-weight[i-1]] + value[i-1]). 3. Return dp[n][W]. Time: O(n*W). Space: O(n*W). Connection to L2: This recurrence shows row dependency enabling compression. Common mistakes include off-by-one in indices or missing base cases. Real code pattern: dp = [[0]*(W+1) for _ in range(n+1)]; for i in range(1,n+1): for w in range(W+1): if w < weights[i-1]: dp[i][w] = dp[i-1][w]; else: dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], dp[i-1][w-weights[i-1]] + values[i-1]); return dp[n][W].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Take or Skip Decision with Max
ladders.desc: For a given capacity, decide whether to take an item by comparing values.
ladders.explanation: This operation is the fundamental decision in knapsack: given a current best value without an item (skip_value) and a candidate value if we take the item (take_value), choose the maximum. Take_value is computed as best value at reduced capacity plus the item's value, but only if the item's weight does not exceed capacity. Intuition: At each step, we greedily select the option that gives higher value, ensuring optimal substructure. Step-by-step: 1. Check if item weight <= capacity. 2. If yes, compute take_value = best_value[capacity - weight] + value. 3. Set best_value[capacity] = max(skip_value, take_value). 4. If weight > capacity, best_value[capacity] = skip_value. Time: O(1) per decision. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is applied in the DP recurrence. Common mistakes include forgetting the weight check or incorrect indexing. Real code pattern: if w >= weight: dp[w] = max(dp[w], dp[w-weight] + value).

examples.input: capacity=5, weight=2, value=7, skip_value=10, best_value array: [0,3,6,9,12,15]
examples.trace: Check: weight=2 <= capacity=5. take_value = best_value[5-2] + 7 = best_value[3] + 7 = 9 + 7 = 16. skip_value = 10. max(10,16) = 16. So new best value at capacity 5 is 16.
Check: weight=4 > capacity=3, so cannot take item. new best = skip_value = 8.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Iteration and Max Function
ladders.desc: Iterate over arrays and use max to find the larger of two numbers.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves basic array manipulation and comparison. Arrays are ordered collections of items; here, we use them to store values like weights or DP states. Iteration means looping through array elements, often with for loops. The max function returns the larger of two numbers, which is essential for making optimal choices in knapsack. Intuition: By comparing values, we can decide which option is better. Step-by-step: 1. Define an array, e.g., arr = [a0, a1, ..., an]. 2. Iterate: for i in range(len(arr)): access arr[i]. 3. Use max: result = max(x, y) where x and y are numbers. Time: O(n) for iteration, O(1) for max. Space: O(n) for array storage. Connection to L4: This is used to implement the take/skip decision. Common mistakes include off-by-one in loops or misunderstanding max behavior. Real code pattern: arr = [1,2,3]; for i in arr: print(i); a = max(5, 3) # a=5.

examples.input: Array [3,1,4,2], iterate and find max value
examples.trace: Start max_val = first element 3. Iterate: i=1: compare max(3,1)=3; i=2: compare max(3,4)=4; i=3: compare max(4,2)=4. Final max=4.

examples.input: Use max to decide between two options: value_without=10, value_with=15
examples.trace: Compute chosen = max(10, 15) = 15.
